subject: Nuns at the Pannonhalma Benedictine Archabbey, Hungary, 1943
context_for_lesson: A 1943 photograph of religious sisters at the Pannonhalma Benedictine Archabbey, the historic Hungarian Catholic monastic site in northwestern Hungary. While Judy was hidden in Pincehely (about 230 km southeast of Pannonhalma) and the religious community there was different, this is the most pedagogically appropriate Hungarian Catholic religious-life image available under explicit Creative Commons license — taken in Hungary, in the same year (1943) when Catholic religious houses across Hungary were continuing their daily life one year before Judy would enter the Catholic world as Ilona. The photograph anchors what a Hungarian Catholic religious community looked like in the wartime period, a useful visual context for the lesson's reading anchors (pages 5-6) about Judy's life with the Ursuline sisters.
publisher_or_host: Wikimedia Commons (originally from FORTEPAN historical photo archive, donor: Rados Tamás OSB)
author: FOTO:Fortepan / Rados Tamás OSB
license_summary: CC BY-SA 3.0 — free to use, share, and adapt with attribution; derivative works must use the same license
attribution_required: Photo: FORTEPAN / Rados Tamás OSB via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 3.0)
lesson_use_intent: Page 5 or 6 (reading anchors when Judy becomes Ilona at the Catholic convent). Caption MUST clarify the religious-order distinction: 'Hungarian nuns at the Pannonhalma Benedictine Archabbey, 1943. Judy was hidden by Ursuline sisters in Pincehely, a different religious community in a different part of Hungary. But the daily rhythm of life — prayer, work, simple meals, plain habits — looked similar across Hungary's Catholic religious houses during the war.'
concerns: Order distinction (Benedictine vs Ursuline) must be acknowledged in the caption, but pedagogically the imagery serves: it shows real Hungarian nuns in 1943, the closest visual context the lesson will get. The men in the original Fortepan archive description are noted as Rados Tamás OSB (the donor, a Benedictine monk). The photo itself shows nuns in habits.
